methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $50M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 1 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Quantum Brilliance is a Series B quantum computing company founded by Mark Luo that has raised $50M+ to date. The valuation universe comprises 11 quantum computing peers: 3 public companies (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) and 8 private firms including Q-CTRL, Quantinuum, and others.

The peer-median approach is inapplicable due to insufficient same-stage and adjacent-stage comparables within the quantum computing cohort—only one same-stage peer was identified, falling short of the three-point minimum threshold for reliable triangulation. Accordingly, valuation relies on stage-progression methodology, which applies a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical of Series B advancement to the $50M total raised proxy, yielding an implied valuation of $95M.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $81M to $109M, with a median of $95M. This relatively narrow range reflects reliance on a single method rather than true triangulation across multiple approaches. Key uncertainties include the representativeness of the 1.9x Series B multiplier for quantum hardware and software subsectors, the actual equity raised in prior rounds (versus assumed equal distribution across the $50M+), and the absence of strong peer-stage data to benchmark burn rate and capital efficiency. A fuller valuation would require either identification of additional same-stage quantum computing peers or supplementary venture capital fund sizing data to calibrate stage progression rates specific to this sector.
